_meta.description: Editable content for FBX deck. Edit via /admin/ or directly. Slide HTML reads keys via data-content attributes; localStorage overrides this file at runtime. v0.9 = problem-first reorder + backup-intro divider at slot 20.

01-cover.lede: Invest in a franchise-powered, kit-of-parts operating system for factory-built housing — engineered to scale linearly in cost and exponentially in output. Welcome to the next IPO of Housing. Change like this only happens once!

02-trends.kicker: THE PROBLEM - HOW DOes an industry MEET THIS DEMAND!?!
02-trends.trend.1: Sprawl is expensive — capital is shifting to dense micro.
02-trends.trend.2: Urban infill is hot — ADUs and density development.
02-trends.trend.3: Starter homes in high demand — sub-$300K per door.
02-trends.trend.4: Factory demand from large national builders.
02-trends.trend.5: National & California laws now permit factory-built homes.
02-trends.block1.title: Modular is not new.
02-trends.block1.body:
Modular's track record is the definition of insanity.
Time for change!
02-trends.block2.title: Factory production is not new.
02-trends.block2.body: Cars, phones, food — everything is built in a factory. If the trend is smaller, faster, more efficient, the answer is a factory.
02-trends.principle.label: Operating Principle
02-trends.principle.text: R&D and infrastructure spend grows linearly. Output grows exponentially.

03-predecessors.headline.a: They tried to own the factory.
03-predecessors.note: Every previous attempt tried to own design, factory, and construction. That requires hundreds of millions and rarely works. FBX is a software layer on a SIP manufacturer network, distributed to trained constructors. We scale by partnership, not by pouring concrete.
03-predecessors.graveyard.head: Companies That Tried · Same Mistake, Different Logos
03-predecessors.p1.name: Katerra
03-predecessors.p2.name: Connect Homes
03-predecessors.p2.verdict: Pre-designed catalog. Built each home in a captive factory.
03-predecessors.p3.name: Plant Prefab
03-predecessors.p3.verdict: Boutique modular. Couldn't escape architect-led pricing.
03-predecessors.p4.name: Method Homes
03-predecessors.p4.verdict: Boxed customers into pre-set models. Scale never came.
03-predecessors.p5.name: Blokable
03-predecessors.p5.verdict: Owned the factory. Couldn't fund the next one.
03-predecessors.p6.name: Cover
03-predecessors.p6.verdict: Backyard ADUs only. Vertical from clay to crew.
03-predecessors.m1.title: They tried to own the whole vertical.
03-predecessors.m1.body: Design, factory, and construction under one roof. $100M+ in capex before a single home delivers. FBX skips the factory layer entirely — partnerships with existing SIP manufacturers do the work.
03-predecessors.m2.title: They sold a catalog of plans.
03-predecessors.m2.body: Lindal Cedar, Deltec, every modular brand — all forced customers to pick from a fixed menu. That's the boxed-in feeling that kills the category. FBX inverts this with a moodboard-first sandbox.
03-predecessors.punchline: Software-on-top, partnership-scaled, moodboard-first. None of the predecessors had any of the three.
03-predecessors.footer.left: Capital-light · Manufacturer-agnostic · Customer feels in control · Constraint-aware by design

04-thesis.p1.label: First Mile
04-thesis.p1.title: AI-Assisted Initiation
04-thesis.p1.body: Marketing, design, sales, permits — vertically integrated. Think Tesla, Carmax, Carvana, but for a delivered home.
04-thesis.p2.label: Warehouse · Distribution Hub
04-thesis.p2.title: Aggregate the kit.
04-thesis.p2.body: Regulatory approvals, structural panels, bath pods, SIPs — sourced, warehoused, and shipped to the Last Mile at scale.
04-thesis.p3.label: Last Mile Network
04-thesis.p3.title: Deliver the home.
04-thesis.p3.body: A trained, equipped franchise network of regional assemblers and installers — the layer no one else has built.

05-opportunity.lede: Spool up, empower, and equip small capable craftsmen within 150 miles of every delivery market in America - help them build in factories. Then they scale - we empower them!
05-opportunity.quote.text:
"A 'HomeFab' is the layer
the rest of the industry
forgot to build."
05-opportunity.card1.title: Franchisee Model
05-opportunity.card1.body: Capital, systems, orders, and support — delivered to small builders who want to scale within their region.
05-opportunity.card2.title: Approved Installers
05-opportunity.card2.body: A trained, vetted sub-contractor network — empowered to install product and care for the customer.
05-opportunity.card3.title: Logistics
05-opportunity.card3.body: Equipment and movers trained to place panels and pods exactly where installers need them, when they need them.
05-opportunity.card4.title: On-Site Assembly Unlock
05-opportunity.card4.body: Systems that allow 100% of land — including infill and difficult sites — to be served by prefabricated product.

06-solution.note: The retail market signals demand. FBX Core ingests it, standardizes it into a kit, and ships it to the Home-Fab. The Last Mile installs. Each layer is a distinct discipline — but they only work as one operating system.
06-solution.c1.title: 1st Mile
06-solution.c1.s1.lab: Customer Desires · Served by AI Software
06-solution.c1.s1.b2: Ease of design — guided, not gated
06-solution.c2.title: FBX Core
06-solution.c2.s1.name: Wall/Roof Panels & Bath/Kitchen Pods
06-solution.c2.s2.name: Create the Kit of Parts
06-solution.c2.s3.name: Create the "Home-Fab" Network
06-solution.c3.title: Last Mile
06-solution.c3.s3.b1: Training simplicity — kit assembly, not bespoke build
06-solution.callout.text: People want to trust the brand but want the uniqueness of iterative design. Automobiles cracked this fifty years ago — standardized platforms, configurable trims. FBX is the same idea, applied to housing.

07-first-mile.subhead: Established. Software enhanced design with panels, vertically integrated sales, and customer care. Think Tesla, Carmax, Carvana — for a delivered home.
07-first-mile.fn1.label: Function · A
07-first-mile.fn1.title: Design
07-first-mile.fn1.body: Leverages rules of panels for product standardization but allows for customer desires - all very fast.
07-first-mile.fn2.label: Function · B
07-first-mile.fn2.title: Systems & Approvals
07-first-mile.fn2.body: Leverages systems, regulatory hurdles, and engineering efficiency.
07-first-mile.fn3.label: Function · C
07-first-mile.fn3.title: Home Sales
07-first-mile.fn3.body: Leverages domain expertise and reduced product variation.
07-first-mile.fn4.label: Function · D
07-first-mile.fn4.title: Permits
07-first-mile.cta.label: Objective
07-first-mile.cta.text: Hand the Last Mile a steady stream of homes to build & support.
07-first-mile.footer.left: Pricing controlled · Customer experience maximized · Tech-leveraged

08-wedge.note: Every previous SIP company treated design as upstream and panel cutting as downstream. We flip that — the customer designs in a sandbox where every move is already SIP-feasible. They never see an option that can't be built.
08-wedge.old.title: Design first. Hand off. Rework half of it.
08-wedge.old.body: The architect designs whatever they want and hands a PDF to the SIP manufacturer. Half the design has to be reworked because it can't be paneled efficiently. Schedule slips, margin evaporates, and the customer feels the friction.
08-wedge.new.title: Design inside the grammar. Always feasible.
08-wedge.new.body: Every move the customer makes is already SIP-feasible — checked against panel grammar, manufacturer specs, and site logistics in real time. No rework, no surprises, no "we can't actually build that."
08-wedge.grammar.title: The SIP grammar the system enforces:
08-wedge.rule2.text: 8'–12' typical, up to 24' with engineered splines
08-wedge.rule3.text: Windows & doors that respect panel layout
08-wedge.footer.left: Software-layer wedge · Constraint-coupled design · No vertical integration · Capital-efficient

09-pipeline.kicker: Real Orders. Real Volume. Already Sold.
09-pipeline.lede: 243 buildings in the per-unit waterfall — $30.9M of revenue delivering across the next twelve months. The large California projects extend the book to 599 buildings through 2028.
09-pipeline.featured.head: Featured Products · ADU & Townhomes
09-pipeline.adu.name: ADU
09-pipeline.mf.name: Townhomes
09-pipeline.fullmix.head: Full Pipeline Mix · 599 Buildings
09-pipeline.row1.label: Townhomes
09-pipeline.row2.label: ADU
09-pipeline.row3.label: Duplex
09-pipeline.row4.label: SFR
09-pipeline.footer.left: Source · Jobs Waterfall v2 · Per-unit detail Jun 2026 → May 2027 · aggregate 2027–28

10-kit-of-parts.lede: A standardized, factory-built component system — specified by software, staged at scale, and delivered to regional Home-Fabs ready for assembly.
10-kit-of-parts.products: Products: ADUs · Townhomes · Duplexes · SFR · Bathroom Pods · SIPs
10-kit-of-parts.part1.label: Component · 01
10-kit-of-parts.part1.title: Software-Aided BOM
10-kit-of-parts.part1.body: The constraint-aware design generates a complete bill of materials — every panel, pod, fastener, and finish — auto-quoted across qualified suppliers.
10-kit-of-parts.part2.label: Component · 02
10-kit-of-parts.part2.title: Structural Panels
10-kit-of-parts.part2.body: AI-assisted, pre-cut panels — field installed by trained crews with engineered placement guides.
10-kit-of-parts.part3.label: Component · 03
10-kit-of-parts.part3.title: Bathroom Pods
10-kit-of-parts.part3.body: The technical, plumbing-heavy work — built in a controlled factory environment, dropped in place on site.
10-kit-of-parts.part4.label: Component · 04
10-kit-of-parts.part4.title: Warehousing
10-kit-of-parts.part4.body: Cost efficiency at scale — aggregated procurement and regional staging across the FBX Core network.
10-kit-of-parts.part5.label: Component · 05
10-kit-of-parts.part5.title: Distribution
10-kit-of-parts.part5.body: Kit of parts shipped from regional warehouse to the Home-Fab — sequenced for just-in-time assembly, no double-handling.
10-kit-of-parts.objective.label: Objective
10-kit-of-parts.objective.text: Regulatory clearance · Efficient design · Best product · Best distribution.

11-distribution.quote.text:
"Distribution excellence is the key.
Bring in parts, package, ship to the
Last Mile — let them earn on the
efficiency we created."

12-projection.kicker: As California Goes, So Goes the Nation
12-projection.law.note: CA leads on housing reform — Title 24 → IECC, ADU laws → 30+ states. Duplex by-right is next, and tight-density markets nationally will follow. Compounding 76% YoY off our 2027 confirmed peak lands at 25,000 units in 2035 — the demand-side mirror of the Expansion Map's 137-fab supply plan.
12-projection.p1.l: YoY Demand Growth · Reverse-engineered from the supply target
12-projection.p2.l: Year-10 Demand · CA pattern, gone national
12-projection.p3.l: Demand validates the plan. Implied fab count = Expansion Map plan
12-projection.footer.left: Source · 599 contracted buildings · 76% YoY off 2027 peak (277) · Mix anchored on confirmed pipeline

13-economics.core.name: Core
13-economics.fab.name: Fab-Home
13-economics.tot.label: Network Totals at Y10

14-ask.sub:
We're raising $4M for FBX Core.
Each Fab-Home franchise is funded independently — $900K franchise fee, $450K infrastructure. The model scales linearly in capital, exponentially in delivery.
14-ask.n1.label: FBX Core · Series A
14-ask.n2.label: Per Franchise · Fee
14-ask.n3.label: Per Franchise · Infrastructure

15-catalyst.note: The Core check pays back from contracts in hand. Then it catalyzes the franchise rollout — 137 Home-Fabs deploying $123.3M in franchise capital across the network. Total cash returned to Series A: $80M at 22% IRR over the 15-year horizon.
15-catalyst.s1.title: Contracted book delivers.
15-catalyst.s1.lab: Core Op Profit · 599 Buildings · By Mid-2028
15-catalyst.s1.body: $4M Series A funds Core operations through delivery of the already-signed pipeline. Returns 3.2× the raise before any franchise activity.
15-catalyst.s2.title: Franchise rollout activates.
15-catalyst.s2.body: Every Home-Fab is independently funded by its franchisee — but each one runs on Core's kit, royalty, and systems. The $4M Core seed unlocks the entire network.
15-catalyst.s3.title: $4M becomes $80M.
15-catalyst.s3.lab: Total Cash Returned · 22% IRR Over Hold
15-catalyst.s3.body: The contracted floor de-risks the entry. The franchise rollout drives the upside. Core's Year-10 economics ($3.23B cumulative profit) translates the catalyst into investor cash returned.
15-catalyst.m3.l: Total Return · 20× the Series A Check
15-catalyst.footer.left: Floor: 599 contracted bldgs × $21,607 Core profit/home = $12.9M · Catalyst: 137 fabs × $900K franchise fee = $123.3M · Outcome: $4M × 1.22^15 = $80M

16-floor.note: 599 buildings already under contract deliver before the franchise rollout begins. At Core's plan-economics ($21,607 op profit per home from slide 13), the contracted book alone returns 3.2× the $4M raise by mid-2028 — before a single new fab is added.
16-floor.hero.raise.lab: FBX Core · Series A · Single Check
16-floor.mix.head: Mix of the 599-Building Contracted Book
16-floor.mix.total: $80.8M total revenue · $51.7M Core revenue · $12.9M Core profit
16-floor.mix.row1.name: Townhomes
16-floor.mix.row2.name: ADU
16-floor.mix.row3.name: Duplex
16-floor.mix.row4.name: SFR
16-floor.footer.left: Core unit economics from slide 13 plan ($86,293 rev/home · $21,607 op profit/home · 25% margin) applied to 599 contracted buildings

17-precedent.lede: The companies investors point to as having "unbeatable moats" today all started with raw speed and distribution. The defensibility came later — built on top of scale they earned by moving first.
17-precedent.co1.name: Amazon
17-precedent.co1.momentum: Cheapest, fastest book selection on the web. Loss-leader pricing to capture share.
17-precedent.co1.moat: Economies of scale. Massive buying volume → cost advantage no competitor can match.
17-precedent.co2.name: Airbnb
17-precedent.co2.momentum: Aggressive host onboarding, design-led trust, viral growth in cities one by one.
17-precedent.co2.moat: Network effects. Hosts and guests reinforce each other — a two-sided moat that compounds.
17-precedent.co3.name: Booking.com
17-precedent.co3.momentum: Billions in performance marketing. Repeat usage. Relentless conversion optimization.
17-precedent.co3.moat: Brand trust. The default verb for booking travel — built one transaction at a time.
17-precedent.co4.name: FBX HomeFab
17-precedent.co4.momentum: First-mover franchise rollout. AI-assisted permits. Hub-and-spoke from Day 1.
17-precedent.co4.moat: All four. Network effects across franchisees, scale economics on the kit, brand on the customer side, embedding through systems.

18-four-moats.source.note: a16z identified four defensibilities that matter most. Each requires scale — and momentum is what unlocks that scale. FBX is engineered for all four.
18-four-moats.moat1.title: Network Effects
18-four-moats.moat1.def: Value grows as more participants join the network.
18-four-moats.moat1.app: More franchisees means denser delivery coverage, more installer capacity, and richer market data — which makes every additional franchise more valuable than the last.
18-four-moats.moat2.title: Economies of Scale
18-four-moats.moat2.def: Costs drop and pricing power grows with volume.
18-four-moats.moat2.app: Aggregated procurement of panels, pods, and SIPs across 137 fabs unlocks pricing no regional builder can match. Hub-and-spoke logistics compound the advantage.
18-four-moats.moat3.title: Brand
18-four-moats.moat3.def: Trust and familiarity built through repeated customer exposure.
18-four-moats.moat3.app: A standardized customer experience across every delivery market — Tesla / Carmax for housing. Repeat exposure turns "factory home" into a category we own.
18-four-moats.moat4.title: Embedding
18-four-moats.moat4.def: Becoming hard to replace through depth of integration.
18-four-moats.moat4.app: Franchisees run on FBX systems — design, permits, kit, logistics, training. Switching out is replacing the whole operating stack, not just a supplier.
18-four-moats.footer.left: Each moat compounds with scale · Scale comes from momentum

19-momentum.lede: Defensibility doesn't exist on Day 0 — it emerges after a startup has already grown. Move fast, gain users, build leverage. The moat shows up once the conditions for it actually exist.
19-momentum.step1.title: Momentum
19-momentum.step1.body: Speed, growth, attention. Adoption loops. Fast iteration. The narrative.
19-momentum.step2.title: Adoption
19-momentum.step2.body: Users committing. First-mile contracts signed. Repeat behavior emerging.
19-momentum.step3.title: Scale
19-momentum.step3.body: Volume hits the threshold where unit costs drop and leverage compounds.
19-momentum.step4.title: The Moat.
19-momentum.step4.body: Defensibility emerges from accumulated scale. Now we're hard to dislodge.
19-momentum.reframe1.label: Momentum
19-momentum.reframe1.text: Short-term speed, growth, attention. Gets you noticed and funded.
19-momentum.reframe2.label: Defensibility — the Moat
19-momentum.reframe2.text: Long-term protection from competitors. Keeps you alive once everyone else notices.

20-backup-intro.lede: What follows is the deeper material — the operating truths, the system layers, the projection model, and the standard objections. Pulled forward when asked.
20-backup-intro.list.label: In this section

21-truths.eyebrow.right: 21 · Strategy · What Has To Be True
21-truths.note: A constraint-aware design platform is the wedge. But it only matters if four operational layers actually work. None are research problems. All are solvable. None should be hand-waved.
21-truths.t1.title: Code & stamping.
21-truths.t1.body: Output has to be stamped by licensed engineers, jurisdiction by jurisdiction. Not a feature flag — a real operations layer with PE partnerships in each target state.
21-truths.t1.verdict.text: Start in 3–5 permissive states. Expand on demand.
21-truths.t2.title: Manufacturer abstraction.
21-truths.t2.body: Each SIP manufacturer has its own panel specs, splines, and tolerances. One design file → 2–3 manufacturer quotes. The kit-of-parts standardization the industry never bothered to build.
21-truths.t3.title: Transport & crane planning.
21-truths.t3.body: A design that's perfect on paper but needs a crane the local rental yard doesn't carry is a failed design. The constraint solver has to know logistics, not just geometry.
21-truths.t4.title: Builder network.
21-truths.t4.body: Even a perfect kit needs a crew that can erect it. The Last Mile franchise (slides 05, 11) is exactly this layer — vetted installers and GCs who learn the system and own the customer relationship.
21-truths.t4.verdict.text: Already in the deck · Last Mile network
21-truths.frame.text: None of these are research problems. All are solvable with operations rigor and a focused rollout. This is what we've already started building.
21-truths.footer.left: We know what we don't know · Operations-first execution · Fault-tolerant rollout sequence

24-workforce.subhead: Behind every structure is a diverse team — engineers, laborers, surveyors, project managers. They want to scale. They demand support. We empower their rise.
24-workforce.stat1.label: Franchise Service Radius
24-workforce.stat2.label: Fabs at Year 10 (national)
24-workforce.stat3.label: States Covered (Year 10)
24-workforce.stat4.label: Business Units · One Outcome

25-objections.obj1.q: How big can this get? Homes per year?
25-objections.obj1.a: Review https://https://homefab-map.onrender.com/. The plan is 149,490 homes delivered by Year 10 at a 43,980/yr run-rate. We can dial it more aggressive or more conservative — the model recalcs live.
25-objections.obj2.q: What is your moat? How do you fend off copycats?
25-objections.obj2.a: Wrong question at this stage. See slides 17–19 — moats emerge from scale, and scale comes from momentum. We're engineered for all four a16z defensibilities (network effects, scale economics, brand, embedding) once we hit volume. First-mover speed is the strategy.
25-objections.obj3.q: Are you national, or competing with Lennar / Champion?
25-objections.obj3.a: Adding to the market — demand is enormous. Our edge is speed and cost of capital, which is why the moat matters more than the comparison.
